Whether you love or hate AI, we can all agree the slot machine nature of generative models sucks. Creators need control -- and perhaps the best way to get it is with 3d tools.

But the first time you open blender, it’s overwhelming. Like staring at a 747 cockpit with a dizzying number of buttons. And even when you become a seasoned pilot -- you absolutely wish you had autopilot for the mundane bits.

Connectors for AI models like claude were supposed to be the best of both worlds -- like having an expert python coder sitting next to you, driving blender through its API while you get complete visibility and control.

Unlike generative image/video models, you’re not surrendering to output you can’t precisely modify, inspect, or understand. You’re directing it to use blender for you. Truly the best of both worlds.

But alas the announcement resulted in instant backlash, and the patronage got pulled by Friday…

The connector still ships -- that part survives. What didn’t survive though was the brand association between the Blender Foundation and Anthropic.

The trust deficit runs so deep that even the most thoughtful, opt in, inspectable AI integration can’t be publicly tied to an AI lab.

Disincentivizing even beneficial applications to 3d creation feels like a real loss -- but gosh, i can imagine the backlash has been overwhelming for the blender team.

Some tell me this is oil and water -- never the twain shall meet. Perhaps or maybe it’s just a matter of time. One thing I’m certain of is that before it gets better, it’ll probably get worse.

Using AI is like wearing an iron man suit for the mind. The augmentation is real, but lean on it long enough and the muscles underneath start to atrophy. 

I think we’ll start writing and thinking manually again - with weekly recommended ranges to avoid brain rot. The cognitive version of hitting the gym in a world where machines do most of the lifting.

Here's an oldie but goodie.. feels more relevant than ever with Nvidias real-time neural rendering, Runways near real-time video gen, all the amazing progress in gaussian splatting research.

I made this a year ago and it's an example of how storytelling even in the time we're moving into, can still be inspiring, fun, creative and controllable. 

Most of the input in the video was made with Dreams (ps4/5 game engine).

Hard to argue that Generative Ai won't radically change how we tell stories and immerse an audience in experiences.

Let's find the best ways to use it to augment the creative process instead of it being a one button solution. 

Impressive work by Dustin. The consistency and visual coherence through the whole thing is particularly impressive. The joker moment here would be him telling us it was made by his agentic system 😅

This is real-time.. 

The debate about Nvidia's latest neural rendering tech is ongoing. One side sees the potential of the technology to bridge the gap across the uncanny valley, to give us a much less rigid creative process than a to b to c.. the other side shouts "slop" and tries to shame any creative who dares to explore this new technology, any chance they get.

Meanwhile, I'm over here saying "I told you so".. Gen Ai is here to stay, its becoming real-time and Nvidia now has the "RTX on" for AI which I've been talking about for years.

We need creatives to help shape meaningful tools that combine this tech with much more granular creative control.. with tools where you as an artist see your creative thumbprint from beginning to end in the parts of the work that matters the most to you. 

If you're shaming artists or storytellers who embrace these tools, you're doing them a disservice in my opinion.

If they don't learn to utilize these tools and workflows, to make them their own and to shape them, others will. 

There's no way this tech won't be a huge part of the creative industry and of the way we tell stories. It's happening around us as we speak.

That said, at the core of it, do what makes you happy and create in any way you like. Don't let people tell you that "you can't do this" or "you have to do it like this".

Back in 2022 I was contacted by Toke Stub Barter about possibly working with him on a pitch for a game. A game about a young woman, looking for her lost twin sister in a strange, dreamlike place between this world and something else.

The protagonist finds herself on an escalator, going down to an underground train station, with no recollection of how she got there. She has a feeling she's been there before, many times.. and that she's looking for something important.

Set in a cinematic, nordic-noir world the idea of the game immediately appealed to me and together with a few other team members, we set out to build the pitch material. The initial, playable prototype that secured us our first rounds of funding, was made entirely in Dreams on the PS5, everything sculpted, painted and animated there from scratch with just my two move controllers.

A few years later I've moved on to work on other projects, but Toke and his band of game developers, have been tinkering away on this gem and it's now out on Steam for you to Wishlist.

I highly recommend this one as a project to follow.

https://t.co/WnIOsWg63M

As if we needed any more proof of what an absolutely incredible achievement Dreams is amongst 3d software and game engines  that all seems to agree, that world building in 3d is an engineering task moreso than a creative endeavor. 

All made on a Playstation - every asset, interaction and game mechanic.
Yes.
I wonder what this is..!?
Dreams.. and nightmares! Look at this - created entirely on a gaming console - all models, textures, animations. Incredible.

So my latest post got a lot of negative attention (not very surprising), so support from guys like Andre, who knows what he is talking about, is very welcome. 

The negative reactions honestly don't affect me much. Of course I'd prefer it to be less but I know that I am in this with no hidden agendas and with an intent to help steer the development in a good direction.  

Listen, I am not a proponent of excluding the human from the creative process. Not by far. I love human creativity, and in my own life, it's my second big love and passion outside of my family. 

I'll always be sculpting, painting, drawing, and creating with any tools and workflows. I have picked up a pencil and spent way too much time drawing the worlds I have in my mind, I have done my due diligence with traditional creative software. I've been sculpting and painting "manually" in VR and with more novel, "weird" tools like Dreams. 

And for the last two years (almost) I've been working with some of the most brilliant people in the world when it comes to AI, creativity, and society at Google. Here I've had the chance to work with people from Hollywood like Darren Aronofsky, Eliza Mcnitt and with former Pixar talent now working with us.

We are in a time now where Generative AI and AI systems in general are an indisputable part of the world we live in. Should we as creatives just lean back and wait for full automation of the creative processes? Should we just pretend that this thing is going to go away again? 

Should we accept that comfy UI and wired nodes with text prompt in them, is the way we'll create going forward? 

Or should we try and approach this thing as new brushes, new paint on our palette, and new ways for us to tell stories and build worlds? 

I know which path I'm choosing. 

I've been walking that path for the last few years now, and I'm not discouraged by what I'm seeing. Quite the contrary. But there is still a lot for us to do in terms of shaping this future. 

I believe that we as creatives can continue to raise the roof in terms of what's possible. We'll continue to push the limits, continue to do surprising things with new tools, and continue to do so in the ways that make most sense to us.

I look forward to a time in the very near future where more of my peers and more of the incredibly talented creative people out there are in this together, and together help create incredible new workflows and tools to do things we could even dream of yesterday ❤️

Would love to hear your thoughts on this, both If you agree or disagree with what I wrote here. I'm curious to hear if you think that creativity at some point will be "solved", and we'll just have a one-button solution for creating. Or if you believe, like I do, that human creativity will always continue to be valuable and something special.
